summary:
The Dreamtime — known by various names across hundreds of Australian Aboriginal language groups — is the foundational cosmological and spiritual framework through which Aboriginal Australians understand the creation, structure, and meaning of the world. During the period 10,000–8,000 BCE, as Australia's climate shifted with the end of the last glacial period and rising seas progressively inundated the continental shelf, Aboriginal communities maintained and elaborated the Dreaming traditions that had been developing since the first human settlement of the continent some 50,000–65,000 years earlier. The flooding of vast coastal lowlands during this period — areas that had been inhabited and storied for millennia — may have contributed to oral traditions and cultural memories preserved in Dreamtime narratives.

The Dreamtime encompasses ancestor beings who shaped the landscape, established the laws of human conduct and kinship, and whose spiritual power remains present in sacred sites, landforms, and living species. It is not conceived as a past era but as an ever-present spiritual reality coexisting with the everyday world. The traditions encoded in Dreamtime narratives regulated land use, resource management, ceremony, and social organization in ways that allowed Aboriginal societies to sustain themselves in diverse Australian environments for tens of thousands of years. The period 10,000–8,000 BCE falls within this long continuity and represents one moment in the unbroken development of the world's oldest continuous living cultural tradition.
